title: Pronouncing Numbers and WH-Words
learningGoal: The learner will master the phonetic distinction between 'TH' sounds (Three, Thirty) and appropriately stress '-TEEN' versus '-TY', while perfectly executing the soft 'W' in question words.
instructions: In English, 13 and 30 sound very similar, but they have a different 'stress' (accentuation). Listen to how the native speaker emphasizes the end of Thir-TEEN, and the beginning of THIR-ty. Replicate this with your microphone.

contentItems.text: For the 'W' sound in What, Where, When—do NOT bite your lip like a 'V'. Make your lips round like you are kissing the air.

targetPhrasesOrWords.phoneticHint: Soft 'Th' (थ). Put your tongue between your teeth.
Stress the THIR. THIR-tee. The end is very short.
Stress the FOR. FOR-tee. Do not pronounce the 'u' (it is not four-ty).
Stress the TEEN. fif-TEEN. Notice it is 'fif', not 'five'.
Stress the FIF. FIF-tee. Short ending.
The 'W' is completely silent! Sounds like an owl: Hoo.
Round lips. Wher. Do not say 'Ver'.
Round lips. Wen. Do not say 'Ven'.
Question intonation: the pitch goes UP at the end of the sentence.
Smoothly link them. 'Izz-she'. Pitch goes UP at the end.

expectedPronunciationText: Three Thirteen Thirty Fourteen Forty Fifteen Fifty Eighty Hundred Who What Where When Are you Is she

feedbackRules.onPass: Magnificent! You correctly differentiated between the tricky 'TEEN' and 'TY' sounds, and used proper 'W' lip shapes.
feedbackRules.onFail: Keep practicing! You might be pronouncing 'W' with a 'V' sound (biting your lip). Round your lips! Also, make sure 13 and 30 don't sound the same.
feedbackRules.onPartial: Good progress. Pay special attention to 'Who'. The 'W' is totally silent. It is just 'Hoo'.

adminCorrectAnswerSet.exactCorrectTranscript: three thirteen thirty fourteen forty fifteen fifty eighty hundred who what where when are you is she
acceptedVariants: who what where when are you is she
adminCorrectAnswerSet.phoneticApproximation: Stress detection is critical for TEEN vs TY differentiation.

hintText: To ask a question properly, your voice should go UP slightly at the very end of the sentence. 'Are YOU?'
commonMistakes: Pronouncing 'When' as 'Ven' (Vocalizing with teeth instead of lips).
